message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T102 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T102    Base commit: 1a757b1 (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard 1a757b1` first; confirm internal/device/device.go has the engineLogger + T100's linkup_linux.go (ifUp) before starting.
headline: Emit an actionable diagnostic on TUN write EIO instead of the raw input/output error (I3)
description: Where the TUN write error surfaces (the engine's tun read/write loop errors reach engineLogger in internal/device/device.go ~L687-697, and/or wrap tun.Device with a thin decorator), detect EIO, inspect the interface state (IFF_UP flags, MTU) and emit an actionable ERROR naming the probable cause and remedy, e.g. 'wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)'. Rate-limit so a write storm produces one diagnostic, not a flood. Keep the raw errno in the record for debugging.
acceptance: Unit test injecting EIO against a fake/DOWN TUN asserts the log record names the interface state and points at install.md §4, with the raw errno included, and that a burst of EIOs yields one rate-limited diagnostic. Relates D39 (diagnoses the D39 symptom) with no dependsOn on its fix. go test ./... green.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Read internal/device/device.go around the engineLogger (~L687) + how the tun read/write loop reports errors; T100 added linkup_linux.go (ifUp via SIOCGIFFLAGS) — reuse that ioctl idiom to inspect IFF_UP/MTU on Linux (with a !linux stub if you add a helper). Mutation-verify the rate-limit (burst → exactly one diagnostic) and the interface-state naming.
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t102-fix` off 1a757b1 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package log
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"io"
6		"log/slog"
7		"strings"
8	)
9	
10	// Field key conventions. Per-path events carry FieldPath so logs can be filtered
11	// by uplink; every component-scoped logger carries FieldComponent.
12	const (
13		FieldComponent = "component"
14		FieldPath      = "path"
15	)
16	
17	// Logger is the structured-logging surface the rest of wanbond depends on, so no
18	// package imports log/slog directly. It supports component- and path-scoped
19	// child loggers whose scope attributes are attached to every subsequent record.
20	type Logger interface {
21		Debug(msg string, args ...any)
22		Info(msg string, args ...any)
23		Warn(msg string, args ...any)
24		Error(msg string, args ...any)
25		// Component returns a child logger tagged with the given component name.
26		Component(name string) Logger
27		// Path returns a child logger tagged with the given path name.
28		Path(name string) Logger
29	}
30	
31	// slogLogger is the slog-backed implementation of Logger.
32	type slogLogger struct {
33		l *slog.Logger
34	}
35	
36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {
41			return nil, err
42		}
43		h := slog.NewJSONHandler(w, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: lvl})
44		return &slogLogger{l: slog.New(h)}, nil
45	}
46	
47	func parseLevel(level string) (slog.Level, error) {
48		switch strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(level)) {
49		case "", "info":
50			return slog.LevelInfo, nil
51		case "debug":
52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":
54			return slog.LevelWarn, nil
55		case "error":
56			return slog.LevelError, nil
57		default:
58			return 0, fmt.Errorf("log: unknown level %q (want debug|info|warn|error)", level)
59		}
60	}
61	
62	func (s *slogLogger) Debug(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Debug(msg, args...) }
63	func (s *slogLogger) Info(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Info(msg, args...) }
64	func (s *slogLogger) Warn(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Warn(msg, args...) }
65	func (s *slogLogger) Error(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Error(msg, args...) }
66	
67	func (s *slogLogger) Component(name string) Logger {
68		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldComponent, name)}
69	}
70	
71	func (s *slogLogger) Path(name string) Logger {
72		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldPath, name)}
73	}

1	//go:build linux
2	
3	package device
4	
5	import (
6		"fmt"
7	
8		"golang.org/x/sys/unix"
9	)
10	
11	// ifUp sets IFF_UP on the named interface via SIOCSIFFLAGS (see netdevice(7)),
12	// closing the silent-dead-tunnel footgun (I1): a write to a DOWN TUN yields EIO,
13	// and until now nothing brought wanbond0 up — the operator had to run `ip link
14	// set up` out of band. It reads the current flags first (SIOCGIFFLAGS) and ORs in
15	// IFF_UP rather than overwriting the flag word, so whatever OTHER flags the
16	// kernel already set on interface creation (POINTOPOINT, NOARP, MULTICAST, …)
17	// survive untouched. Addressing stays operator-owned; this touches ONLY the
18	// administrative up/down flag, mirroring the golang.org/x/sys/unix ioctl idiom
19	// used for SO_BINDTODEVICE in internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go.
20	func ifUp(name string) error {
21		fd, err := unix.Socket(unix.AF_INET, unix.SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
22		if err != nil {
23			return fmt.Errorf("open control socket: %w", err)
24		}
25		defer func() { _ = unix.Close(fd) }()
26	
27		ifr, err := unix.NewIfreq(name)
28		if err != nil {
29			return fmt.Errorf("build ifreq for %q: %w", name, err)
30		}
31		if err := unix.IoctlIfreq(fd, unix.SIOCGIFFLAGS, ifr); err != nil {
32			return fmt.Errorf("SIOCGIFFLAGS %q: %w", name, err)
33		}
34		ifr.SetUint16(ifr.Uint16() | unix.IFF_UP)
35		if err := unix.IoctlIfreq(fd, unix.SIOCSIFFLAGS, ifr); err != nil {
36			return fmt.Errorf("SIOCSIFFLAGS %q: %w", name, err)
37		}
38		return nil
39	}
40

8	// Linux; this stub only keeps the package cross-compilable, mirroring
9	// internal/bind/pathsock_other.go.

1	// Package device brings a wanbond tunnel up from a validated configuration: it
2	// creates the TUN interface, drives the embedded amneziawg-go engine over the
3	// multipath Bind, and applies the WireGuard (and, when configured, amnezia
4	// obfuscation) parameters via the engine's UAPI. It owns ONLY the tunnel engine
5	// — interface addressing and routing are left to the operator (systemd/wg-quick
6	// style), so the daemon stays free of privileged shell-outs. The interface name
7	// is exposed via Tunnel.Name for that external configuration step.
8	package device
9	
10	import (
11		"context"
12		"crypto/rand"
13		"encoding/hex"
14		"fmt"
15		"net/netip"
16		"reflect"
17		"strings"
18		"sync"
19		"time"
20	
21		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
22		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
23	
24		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
25		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
26		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
27		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
28		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
29		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
30		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
31		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
32		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
33	)
34	
35	// metricsShutdownTimeout bounds the graceful shutdown of the /metrics endpoint on
36	// Close (and on a reload that rebinds it). The endpoint is loopback-only with no
37	// long-lived scrapes, so this is comfortably generous.
38	const metricsShutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
39	
40	// defaultTUNName is the requested interface name; the kernel honours it unless it
41	// collides (it never does across the edge and concentrator network namespaces).
42	const defaultTUNName = "wanbond0"
43	
44	// tunMTU is the tunnel (TUN) MTU for a config: the default path MTU minus the
45	// IP+UDP, outer DATA-frame, and WireGuard transport overheads, so a full-MTU inner
46	// packet never fragments on the wire (see internal/bind mtu.go and docs/p1-mtu.md).
47	// This is smaller than the plain-WireGuard 1420 because the bonding layer adds its
48	// own outer DATA frame per datagram; with FEC enabled it is a further
49	// bind.FECParityMTUPenalty smaller so a full-size PARITY frame also fits the path MTU
50	// (T24) rather than fragmenting.
51	func tunMTU(cfg *config.Config) int {
52		return bind.InnerMTU(bind.DefaultPathMTU, cfg.FEC.Enabled)
53	}
54	
55	// Tunnel is a running wanbond tunnel: the amneziawg engine, its TUN, and the
56	// multipath Bind. Close tears all three down.
57	type Tunnel struct {
58		dev  *awgdevice.Device
59		tun  tun.Device
60		name string
61		// bind is the multipath Bind the engine drives; Reload calls its runtime
62		// AddPath/RemovePath to apply a config-reload path diff (T30).
63		bind *bind.Multipath
64		// cfg is the configuration the RUNNING tunnel reflects: the boot config, its path
65		// set updated on each successful Reload to the current membership (survivors keep
66		// their original parameters — a membership-only reload does not re-apply modified
67		// path parameters or non-path fields). Reload diffs the next desired config against
68		// it to warn about changes it cannot apply. Guarded by reloadMu.
69		cfg *config.Config
70		// reloadMu serializes Reload against itself (SIGHUP handlers), guarding cfg.
71		reloadMu sync.Mutex
72		// log is the device-component logger, retained for Reload diagnostics.
73		log log.Logger
74		// stopProbes halts the per-path probe cadence loop (T37). Close calls it before
75		// tearing the engine down so emission stops before the sockets close. It is
76		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers).
77		stopProbes func()
78		// stopReconcile halts the background deferred-path reconcile loop (T55): it retries
79		// binding the paths a tolerant Open left DOWN as their addresses appear. Close calls
80		// it before tearing the engine down so no reconcile races the socket close. It is
81		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers / never defers).
82		stopReconcile func()
83		// stopHubFailover halts the edge-side hub-failover monitor loop (T57): it watches the
84		// per-path liveness plane and, on hub loss (all paths to the active concentrator DOWN),
85		// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates
86		// a fresh WG re-handshake. Close calls it before tearing the engine down. It is
87		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, or an edge with a single
88		// concentrator endpoint, or a bind without the probe transport).
89		stopHubFailover func()
90		// stopResolution halts the edge-side DNS re-resolution loop (T74): it re-resolves each
91		// opted-in hostname peer-endpoint spec on the [dns] poll cadence and out-of-band on hub loss,
92		// feeding fresh records to the hub-failover controller. Close calls it between stopHubFailover
93		// and dev.Close. It is idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, an all-literal
94		// edge, or when no hostname spec is configured — Q29 inertness).
95		stopResolution func()
96		// stopSession halts the WG-session monitor loop (I2): it polls the engine's last-handshake
97		// state at probe cadence and emits ONE INFO 'session established' record on each 0->1 edge.
98		// Close calls it before dev.Close so no IpcGet races the engine teardown. It is idempotent
99		// and never nil.
100		stopSession func()

180	
181	// release drops an engine's hold on the guard. A configured engine clears the
182	// single configured slot; a plain engine decrements the live plain count.
183	func (g *amneziaGuard) release(a config.Amnezia) {
184		g.mu.Lock()
185		defer g.mu.Unlock()
186		if a.Configured() {
187			g.configLive = false
188			return
189		}
190		if g.plainLive > 0 {
191			g.plainLive--
192		}
193	}
194	
195	// Up creates the TUN, wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine,
196	// applies the crypto configuration from cfg, and brings the device up. The same
197	// path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI fields cfg carries
198	// (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
199	func Up(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (*Tunnel, error) {
200		clg := lg.Component("device")
201	
202		tunDev, err := tun.CreateTUN(defaultTUNName, tunMTU(cfg))
203		if err != nil {
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: create TUN: %w", err)
205		}
206		name, err := tunDev.Name()
207		if err != nil {
208			_ = tunDev.Close()
209			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: read TUN name: %w", err)
210		}
211		// Bring the interface administratively UP now (I1): amneziawg-go never does this
212		// itself, and a write to a DOWN TUN yields EIO — a silent-dead-tunnel footgun the
213		// operator previously had to work around with an out-of-band `ip link set up`.
214		// Addressing stays operator-owned; this sets ONLY the IFF_UP flag (see ifUp).
215		if err := ifUp(name); err != nil {
216			_ = tunDev.Close()
217			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring interface up: %w", err)
218		}
219		// Apply the TUN persistence policy (I7, Q38) BEFORE the amnezia single-engine
220		// guard in up(): with tun_persist=true the kernel keeps wanbond0 (and its
221		// operator-owned addresses/routes) across a daemon restart; with the default
222		// false it explicitly CLEARS the flag so the device disappears on Close exactly
223		// as before. This composes with the ifUp above and does not touch the reload or
224		// restart-on-failure paths — a reload never re-creates the TUN, and a persistent
225		// device is simply re-adopted by name on the next Up (see setTUNPersist).
226		if err := setTUNPersist(tunDev, cfg.TUNPersist); err != nil {
227			_ = tunDev.Close()
228			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: set TUN persistence: %w", err)
229		}
230		clg.Info("interface up", "interface", name, "tun_persist", cfg.TUNPersist)
231		// The DNS re-resolution transport is the one the (validated) [dns] block selects. It is passed
232		// as a factory (not eagerly constructed) so up() builds it AT MOST ONCE and ONLY when some peer
233		// carries a hostname endpoint spec — a config with zero hostname specs never constructs a
234		// resolver (Q29 inertness). The injected TUN and factory are also the seams device tests drive.
235		return up(cfg, clg, tunDev, name, cfg.DNS.NewResolver)
236	}
237	
238	// resolverFactory builds the DNS resolver used for hostname endpoint re-resolution. It is injected
239	// so a device test can supply a fake; production passes cfg.DNS.NewResolver. up() invokes it AT
240	// MOST ONCE, and ONLY when some peer carries a hostname endpoint spec.
241	type resolverFactory func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error)
242	
243	// up drives the tunnel bring-up over an already-created TUN and an injected resolver factory: it
244	// wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine, performs the bounded initial hostname
245	// resolve (Q30), applies the crypto/endpoint UAPI config built ONLY from resolved entries, brings
246	// the device up, and starts the probe / reconcile / hub-failover / re-resolution loops. Splitting
247	// it out of Up gives device tests a seam to inject a channel TUN and a fake resolver without the
248	// privileged tun.CreateTUN. The same path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI
249	// fields cfg carries (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).

250	func up(cfg *config.Config, clg log.Logger, tunDev tun.Device, name string, newResolver resolverFactory) (*Tunnel, error) {
251		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
252		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
253		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
254		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
255		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
256			_ = tunDev.Close()
257			return nil, err
258		}
259		ok := false
260		defer func() {
261			if !ok {
262				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
263			}
264		}()
265	
266		// One random per-boot probe session id, shared by every path AND every configured peer (it
267		// identifies THIS boot, not a path or peer): a peer restart presents a new id that resets
268		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12), and a
269		// runtime-added path (T30) reuses it so its probes join this boot's stream.
270		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
271		if err != nil {
272			_ = tunDev.Close()
273			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: new probe session id: %w", err)
274		}
275		// PeerIdentities is the SINGLE place the single-peer/multi-peer effective-PSK decision is
276		// made (G4): for a single-peer config every identity's PSK is the top-level psk; for a
277		// multi-peer concentrator each is that peer's own psk. Its order matches cfg.WireGuard.Peers
278		// (and thus uapiConfig's peer render), so the engine peer set and the Bind peer set agree.
279		ids := cfg.PeerIdentities()
280		// The primary peer (peers[0]) drives on-wire liveness through the SAME prober values that
281		// back its scheduler's PathHealth (T37). It is keyed on the FIRST identity's effective psk:
282		// the top-level psk for a single-peer config (byte-identical to pre-G4), or peer 0's own psk
283		// for a multi-peer concentrator.
284		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, ids[0].PSK, sessionID, clg)
285		if err != nil {
286			_ = tunDev.Close()
287			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
288		}
289		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, ids[0].PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia)
290		if err != nil {
291			_ = tunDev.Close()
292			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
293		}
294		// Concentrator per-peer wiring (G4/T93): register each ADDITIONAL configured peer with its
295		// OWN prober set, scheduler, and prober factory (all keyed on that peer's effective psk),
296		// BEFORE dev.Up drives the bind's Open — which builds each registered peer's per-(peer,path)
297		// view of every bound socket, reconciles its scheduler, and reports its stable virtual
298		// endpoint to the engine (A1). A single-peer config has exactly one identity, so this loop
299		// is empty and the wiring stays byte-identical to the pre-G4 single-peer path.
300		for _, id := range ids[1:] {
301			psched, pprobers, pfactory, perr := buildScheduler(cfg, id.PSK, sessionID, clg)
302			if perr != nil {
303				_ = tunDev.Close()
304				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler for peer %q: %w", id.Name, perr)
305			}
306			if perr := mpBind.AddConcentratorPeer(id.Name, id.PSK, psched, pprobers, pfactory); perr != nil {
307				_ = tunDev.Close()
308				return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: wire concentrator peer %q: %w", id.Name, perr)
309			}
310		}
311		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level))
312	
313		// Bounded initial hostname resolution (Q30): construct the resolver ONCE (only when some peer
314		// carries a hostname spec — Q29 inertness), resolve each hostname spec under a short timeout,
315		// and seed its expansion. The per-peer boot endpoint — the flattened head of the seeded specs —
316		// is what the UAPI render installs on the engine peer; a hostname that does not resolve in the
317		// boot window (or a resolver that fails to construct) leaves its spec EMPTY and the peer boots
318		// WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot). The re-resolution loop then completes the wiring on
319		// first success via the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70). Literal specs are seeded verbatim, so

687	### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)
688	
689	The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
690	administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
691	so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
692	but owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses or routes**
693	(wg-quick style, no privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a
694	**systemd-networkd `.network` file**, using the inner addresses from
695	`allowed_ips`:
696	
697	```ini
698	# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
699	[Match]
700	Name=wanbond0
701	
702	[Network]
703	Address=10.77.0.2/24
704	```
705	
706	```sh
707	systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
708	```
709	
710	Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
711	drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
712	the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
713	and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
714	with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
715	unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
716	watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
717	appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
718	there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)
719	
720	#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)
721	
722	By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
723	the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
724	it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
725	dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
726	any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.

### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)

The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
but owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses or routes**
(wg-quick style, no privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a
**systemd-networkd `.network` file**, using the inner addresses from
`allowed_ips`:

```ini
# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
[Match]
Name=wanbond0

[Network]
Address=10.77.0.2/24
```

```sh
systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
```

Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)

#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)

By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.

Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to
`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to
being torn down on stop.

> **NetworkManager (D39):** a *persistent* `wanbond0` still needs the
> NetworkManager `unmanaged-devices` drop-in on NM-managed hosts. Persistence
> keeps the link alive across restarts; it does **not** exempt the interface
> from NetworkManager, which would otherwise flush the addresses/routes the
> operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of
> `tun_persist`.

- createdAt: 2026-07-13T22:49:13.912Z
- updatedAt: 2026-07-13T22:49:13.912Z
- author: fable-5
- session: cac93b81-5292-42e3-b77e-962544c75e54
- headline: "wanbond0 left DOWN + unaddressed; NetworkManager flushes the operator's address → cryptic `TUN write: input/output error`"
- severity: medium
- description: "[fixes-doc D5, S2 — production deploy; workaround applied on the Pi.] The daemon creates the tun but never brings the link UP or addresses it. On a NetworkManager host (RPi OS / Debian / Ubuntu desktop — the common edge) the tun is auto-managed: NM FLUSHES the operator's address on link-up and the interface stays DOWN across daemon restarts. Writing into it then yields the cryptic `TUN write: input/output error`. Worked around with NM `unmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0` + an addressing oneshot unit. Note the shipped docs (T22/R27, docs/install.md §4) cover only systemd-networkd addressing — the NM case is undocumented (fixes-doc C1). Fix direction split across: daemon brings the link UP itself (fixes-doc I1, low-risk; addressing stays operator-owned), actionable EIO diagnostics (fixes-doc I3), interface/route persistence across restarts (fixes-doc I7), and the NM + oneshot doc recipes (fixes-doc C1/C4) — the improvements/doc side is tracked in the wanbond-fixes goal; this defect item covers the DOWN-link/NM failure mode itself."
- suggestedFix: "Minimum code fix: bring wanbond0 UP after creating it (link-up only; addressing remains operator-owned), and on TUN write EIO check link state/MTU and emit an actionable error (e.g. 'wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)'). Document NM unmanaged-devices + the PartOf oneshot pattern. Consider keeping the tun persistent across daemon restarts so addresses/routes/rules referencing it survive (I7)."

- ledgerRefs: ["tasks:T22"]
- tags: ["production-deploy","networkmanager","tun"]

+// ifState reads the named interface's administrative up/down flag (SIOCGIFFLAGS) and MTU
+// (SIOCGIFMTU) WITHOUT modifying anything — the read-only counterpart to ifUp, used to name
+// the probable cause when a TUN write fails with EIO (I3/D39): a write to a DOWN interface is
+// the textbook case, but naming the ACTUAL state (rather than assuming) also covers the case
+// where the link is up yet writes still fail (a driver/NIC fault), so the diagnostic never
+// asserts a cause it did not verify.
+func ifState(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error) {
+  fd, err := unix.Socket(unix.AF_INET, unix.SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
+  if err != nil {
+    return false, 0, fmt.Errorf("open control socket: %w", err)
+  defer func() { _ = unix.Close(fd) }()
+  flagsIfr, err := unix.NewIfreq(name)
+  if err != nil {
+    return false, 0, fmt.Errorf("build ifreq for %q: %w", name, err)
+  if err := unix.IoctlIfreq(fd, unix.SIOCGIFFLAGS, flagsIfr); err != nil {
+    return false, 0, fmt.Errorf("SIOCGIFFLAGS %q: %w", name, err)
+  up = flagsIfr.Uint16()&unix.IFF_UP != 0
+  mtuIfr, err := unix.NewIfreq(name)
+  if err != nil {
+    return up, 0, fmt.Errorf("build ifreq for %q: %w", name, err)
+  if err := unix.IoctlIfreq(fd, unix.SIOCGIFMTU, mtuIfr); err != nil {
+    return up, 0, fmt.Errorf("SIOCGIFMTU %q: %w", name, err)
+  mtu = int(int32(mtuIfr.Uint32()))
+  return up, mtu, nil

## Keep the docs current (required)

**Documentation is part of the definition of done.** When a change alters
behavior, configuration, architecture, the wire format, an invariant, or an
operational step, update the affected docs **in the same change**:

- **[README.md](README.md)** — the front door: capabilities, quick start, repo

  verification step or a real-link measurement changes.

A change that moves the code but not the docs it invalidates is **incomplete**.

touched. Prefer editing the existing doc over adding a new one; keep docs
consistent with each other (e.g. a config key described in README, design, and

## 6. Observability

Each daemon serves Prometheus metrics on the loopback-only `[metrics] listen`
address (`curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9090/metrics`). Logs go to stderr →
`journalctl -u wanbond-<role>`.

## 7. MTU

The daemon sets the TUN MTU itself from the bonded-overhead budget (see
`docs/p1-mtu.md`); do not override it. If an on-path MTU below the default
1500 is in play, see the TCP MSS-clamp guidance in that document.

## 8. Limitations

### UDP-blocking networks defeat wanbond (no TCP/TLS fallback — by design)

feat(device): actionable diagnostic on TUN write EIO (I3, T102)

diagnosingTUN wraps the TUN before it reaches the amneziawg engine: on a
Write that fails with EIO it inspects the interface's link state (IFF_UP)
and MTU via a new read-only ifState (linkup_linux.go, mirroring T100's
ifUp ioctl idiom) and logs ONE actionable ERROR naming the probable cause
and remedy (e.g. "wanbond0 is DOWN - address & bring it up (install.md
§4)"), with the raw errno kept alongside for debugging. Rate-limited to
one diagnostic per 30s so a write storm does not flood the log; the
engine's own raw "input/output error" logging is untouched underneath.
Diagnoses the D39 symptom without depending on its fix.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented task T102 in worktree `implement/T102` (rebased onto the correct base 1a757b1 — my worktree's original HEAD was stale by 92 commits, so I `git checkout -b implement/T102 1a757b1` per the sanctioned base-refresh instructions).

- **Discovered:** the raw EIO surfaces from `github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go@v1.0.4`'s `device/receive.go:572` (`device.log.Errorf("Failed to write packets to TUN device: %v", err)`), fed through `engineLogger`'s `Errorf` closure — but that closure only receives a pre-formatted string, not the structured `error`, so intercepting there would mean fragile substring-matching on the vendor's literal message. Wrapping `tun.Device` (the approach the task explicitly allowed as an alternative) is far more robust: `diagnosingTUN.Write` sees the real `error` and can `errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO)` directly. Also reused T100's `ifUp` ioctl idiom to add a read-only `ifState` (SIOCGIFFLAGS + SIOCGIFMTU) in `linkup_linux.go`/`linkup_other.go`.
- **Issues:** none. The rate-limit uses an injectable clock (`now func() time.Time`) and an injectable state probe (`probeState func(string) (bool, int, error)`) so unit tests drive it deterministically without a real/privileged network interface — tests live in the same `device` package and construct `diagnosingTUN` via struct literal, matching the existing test style (`fakeClock` reused from `metrics_test.go`, `syncBuffer`/`log.New(..., buf)` pattern reused from `session_test.go`).
